description: Adding GDPR soft-delete to accounts table. Review the index strategy, uniqueness semantics under soft-delete, and whether the migration adequately supports the application's query patterns.
review_checklist: Identify whether soft-deleted rows break uniqueness constraints
Evaluate partial index opportunities for queries on active rows
Check NULL semantics in soft-delete patterns
Verify tombstone column placement and indexability

ground_truth.description: Re-creating UNIQUE(workspace_id, email) without WHERE deleted_at IS NULL means a user cannot re-register with the same email after soft-deletion — uniqueness applies across all rows including tombstones. Use a partial unique index: `CREATE UNIQUE INDEX ... ON accounts(workspace_id, email) WHERE deleted_at IS NULL`.

Index on deleted_at is wasted space — the column is NULL for 99%+ of rows (active users) and only set when soft-deleted. Replace with a partial index for the actual query: `CREATE INDEX ... WHERE deleted_at IS NULL` (covers active-user queries) or filter on deleted_at IS NOT NULL for admin reports.

Existing query patterns (`SELECT * FROM accounts WHERE workspace_id = ?`) now scan tombstones too. Without partial indexes WHERE deleted_at IS NULL on existing indexes (idx_accounts_workspace, idx_accounts_email), every read pays the deleted-row tax. As deletions accumulate, query performance degrades linearly.

UPDATE without batching on accounts. Even with low write traffic, this acquires row-level locks for the full table. Should be batched, OR the column should have been created with a non-NULL default initially to avoid the UPDATE altogether.

Schema design redundancy: `is_active` boolean and the new `deleted_at` overlap. Either represents account state. Two sources of truth invite drift — application code may set one but not the other. Pick one: `deleted_at IS NULL` is the canonical soft-delete convention; remove `is_active` or document its distinct meaning (e.g. suspended-but-not-deleted).

deletion_reason as VARCHAR(60) is unconstrained free-text. For analytics and compliance reporting, use an enum or CHECK constraint enumerating allowed reasons (user_request, gdpr_erasure, admin_action, etc.).
